A fishing log app built in 4 hours with zero code — testing whether AI tools can replace a dev team for production consumer apps.
Working demo in 4 hours, zero code — March 2026

import BuildTimeline from '../../components/BuildTimeline.tsx'; ## The Thesis Test My core thesis: AI-fluent product leaders can build and ship at a scale that used to require large teams. catch.ai tests a specific piece of that — how far can a prototype actually go? Not just a demo with buttons, but smart integrations, data transformation layers, and real feature depth. Can AI development tools deliver production-grade functionality at prototype speed? ## The Problem Fishing trips generate useful data — location, conditions, what worked, what didn't. I was tracking it across four tools: handwritten notes, Google Maps pins, manually pulled USGS stream data, and ChatGPT for pattern questions. Nothing connected. I couldn't find patterns across trips, and every spot looked the same in my memory. ## The Process I brainstorm on walks, talking through product thinking with ChatGPT. That produces a guidelines file — design and architecture decisions — that becomes the blueprint for Lovable. From there: ~10 prompts to set up the core structure and speech-to-text tooling. Four hours from zero to a working demo. - **Research & scoping:** ChatGPT for feature definition, API exploration, creative brainstorming - **Guidelines file:** Architecture and design decisions captured before touching Lovable - **Build:** Lovable for the full application — UI, backend, data layer. ~10 prompts to working app. - **Zero code written.** Every implementation decision was directed through prompts. ## What It Does **Voice log → AI normalization** — Talk through a trip naturally. The app transcribes and normalizes it into a structured log entry: species, location, conditions, techniques, outcome. No forms. **AI suggestions** — A fishing assistant with access to your log history. More like a knowledgeable guide than a generic chatbot. **USGS data integration** *(in progress)* — Raw API responses from USGS stream gauges translated into plain-language fishing condition summaries.
## What's Actually Hard The 4-hour demo proves the thesis directionally — but the edge cases reveal where AI tooling still has friction: - **Maps integration:** Getting the UI right took more iteration than the core app logic. - **Safari microphone access:** Mobile browser permissions are a pain point that Lovable can't abstract away. - **Security:** Cloudflare configuration to prevent AI endpoint abuse required manual problem-solving outside the AI toolchain. - **USGS database:** The upstream data structure is messy — harder to work with than expected, and not something prompts can easily fix. The pattern: core product logic is fast. Platform integrations, security, and upstream data quality are where the time goes. ## What This Proves (So Far) A single product-minded person with AI tools can get to a working, deployed consumer app in an afternoon. But the more interesting question isn't speed to first demo — it's depth. Can you add smart integrations (USGS stream data → plain-language fishing conditions)? Can you build transformation layers that interpret messy upstream data? Can you ship features like voice-to-structured-data that would normally require a dedicated ML engineer? Early answer: yes, with caveats. The core product logic and AI features are fast. Platform integrations, security, and upstream data quality are where the time goes — and that's where I'm pushing now. ## Try It [catchapp.kyle-mcleod.io](https://catchapp.kyle-mcleod.io)
A manufacturing ERP built by a product leader using Claude skills and AI workflows — testing whether domain experts can replace systems integrators.
Solo Builder — Claude skills + ChatGPT architecture

import BuildTimeline from '../../components/BuildTimeline.tsx'; ## The Thesis Test My core thesis: a product domain expert can recreate the product development and systems integration process with agentic AI tools and workflows. ERPForge tests the sharpest version of that — can you build the *process* for building ERPs, not just one ERP? ## The Problem Traditional ERPs exist because code was expensive. Vendors built lowest-common-denominator software for a business segment, then tried to configure it per customer. Big customers got the most features. Small ones got the base package or cobbled together Excel and point solutions. At MacroFab, we built manufacturing workflows in-house because MRPs were expensive and slow to implement. The cost structure forced that tradeoff — build it yourself or settle for software that doesn't match your operations. What if the cost structure changed? ## The Approach I architected the system with ChatGPT — talked through the design on a walk, generated a `CLAUDE.md` file and an architecture doc — then used Claude to build the full platform in a day. The interesting part isn't the ERP. It's the layer on top: Claude skills for design review, discovery question templates, documentation generation. The skills *are* the product. The ERP is proof the process works. - **`/design-review`** — structured 10-section review before writing any code - **Discovery templates** — 80 questions across 12 categories for BOM and product scoping - **`/write-docs`** — auto-generates API reference, migration guides, and changelogs The BOM rebuild is the clearest example. I gave Claude context on typical BOM structures and manufacturing processes, gave it skills around BOM information, and had it interview me about product workflows and configurations. Then it rebuilt the data model. Four hours from kickoff to a restructured, production-ready BOM system — work that would normally take a systems integrator weeks. ## What It Does Eight integrated modules: Products & BOM, Quotes, Orders, Inventory, Purchasing, Manufacturing, Shipping, and Invoices. The full quote-to-order-to-ship workflow runs end to end. The BOM system is the deepest module — recursive multi-level BOMs with revision control, alternate components, CSV import with column auto-detection, and depth-first cost rollup through the full tree.
## What's Actually Hard Cloud hosting was new territory for me — took a full day to get Railway + Cloudflare configured with containers, DNS, and security. The next challenge: turning manual skills into automated agent workflows. Right now, Claude follows the skills when I prompt it. The goal is a system that runs the full interview → build → iterate loop without manual orchestration. That's the difference between "I can build an ERP fast" and "anyone with domain knowledge can." ## What This Proves (So Far) A domain expert with the right AI workflow can stand up a realistic manufacturing ERP in days. But the bigger bet isn't one ERP — it's whether the skills and templates that built this one can build the next one faster, and eventually build themselves. ## Try It [erpforge.kyle-mcleod.io](https://erpforge.kyle-mcleod.io)
AI development tools are the new leverage — they won't topple every software moat, but they change who gets to attack.

## The Original Barbarians

In the 1980s, leveraged buyouts changed who could buy companies. The deals weren't new — the leverage was. Firms that had no business acquiring billion-dollar companies suddenly could, because new financial instruments gave them access to capital that previously required decades of accumulated wealth.

Most of those firms weren't better operators. Many failed. But enough succeeded to permanently change how corporate America thought about defensibility. The moat wasn't ownership anymore — it was operations.

The same thing is happening to software.

## The New Leverage

AI development tools — Lovable, Cursor, Claude, v0 — are doing to software what junk bonds did to acquisitions. They're not making everyone a software engineer. They're giving people who understand business logic the leverage to build without one.

That distinction matters. The bottleneck in most SaaS platforms was never the individual transaction logic — it's straightforward. The complexity is in encoding thousands of business rules, edge cases, and operational workflows into code. That required teams of engineers who often didn't fully understand the domain, translating requirements from people who did.

Now the people who understand the business logic can write the rules themselves. Not all of them. Not perfectly. But enough to get 70% of the way to a working platform in days instead of months.

And that changes the math on whether to attempt the other 30%.

## What's Actually at Risk

Not everything. Large codebases built on simple individual transactions but layered with operational complexity — quoting engines, workflow automation, approval routing, marketplace matching — these are the castles with thinner walls than their owners think.

The moat was never the code. It was the cost and time required to replicate the code. When that drops by an order of magnitude, the moat is suddenly just the business logic — and the attacker might understand that better than the incumbent's engineering team does.

## What Still Holds

Distribution. Sales teams. Network effects. Regulatory compliance. Data moats built on years of customer activity. DevOps at scale. None of these get easier because someone can prompt their way to a working prototype.

The barbarians of the 1980s still had to operate the companies they bought. Many couldn't. The same will be true here — building the platform is becoming the easy part. Running it, selling it, and scaling it still require everything it always did.

## The Shift

The original barbarians didn't win every deal. They didn't need to. They changed the game by making it possible to *try*. Incumbents who had coasted on the sheer difficulty of replication suddenly had to defend on merit.

That's where software is heading. AI tools won't topple every SaaS company. But they're about to change who gets to take a shot — and how many shots get taken.
Writing Product Specs That Engineers Actually Read
A practical template for PRDs that reduce back-and-forth and accelerate delivery.

## The Problem With Most PRDs

Most product specs are written for the author, not the reader. They're long, they bury the decision, and they leave engineers guessing about what "done" looks like.

Here's a format that works better.

## The One-Page Spec

```
Problem: [one sentence — what user pain are we solving?]
Hypothesis: [if we build X, we expect Y, as measured by Z]
Out of scope: [explicit list of what we are NOT doing]
Success criteria: [specific, measurable, time-bound]
Open questions: [decisions still needed + owner + deadline]
```

That's it. Everything else — wireframes, edge cases, technical notes — goes in appendices that engineers can pull when they need them.

## The Key Principle

**Lead with the problem, not the solution.** Engineers are problem-solvers. Give them the problem first and they'll often suggest solutions better than the one you had in mind.

## What To Skip

- Background sections longer than 2 sentences
- Stakeholder alignment details (put those in a separate doc)
- Exhaustive edge case tables before the core flow is agreed on

Start lean. Expand only when the team asks for more.
